Today we're going to talk about bullets and headlines. And I'm going to try to combine those two together. We talked about the other day how you can have a headline. It could be a bullet. You could have a bullet. It could be a headline. Headlines tend to be a little more general. But sometimes you want to have a, this is a thing about testing, sometimes you want to test a headline that's very general, like the wonderful newsletter that gives you everything you need about your money and your health. But then sometimes it works if you take a bullet that's really specific like what never to eat on an airplane or what an HR manager should never say to an employee they're about to fire. So you see how that could work, how that could become a headline also? So I wanted to talk first a little bit about bullets. Bullets are something that you can use in the copy to really get the reader's curiosity, to really get the reader that they want to get the newsletter. A lot of times it's a special report that we're talking about. A lot of times it may be things in past issues of newsletters. Just getting their curiosity. Bullets are, to a large extent, about curiosity. Like, wow, I got to know what never to eat on an airplane. That's so intriguing. I can't sleep till I find out. I have to get the product in order just to find that out. Bullets should be so powerful that someone could buy the newsletter, buy the book, just to find the answer to that one bullet. That's how powerful they can be sometimes. So there's a couple of things that go into bullets. B, U, L, L, E, T, S. So the first one is B, and that's benefit. There should be some benefit that the reader's got from that. What never to eat on an airplane. That's a big benefit that I won't die from eating whatever bad thing it is that I shouldn't eat on an airplane. How to cure arthritis with a certain herb. How to cure the magic herb that cures arthritis is a benefit there that I get my arthritis pain goes away. So you always want to include some sort of benefit, and you always want to try to have it maybe in the beginning of the bullet. A lot of times people say, save it for the end. You want to put it right up front, because that's what gets the reader into the bullet, into reading it. Unexpected. What never to eat on an airplane is kind of unexpected. I did a bullet once for a book on estate planning. You know what that is? Like when someone dies and they tax your estate, in the United States, and we don't like that. We want to pay as little as possible. We want to leave our money to our children and our grandchildren like we were talking about. It was a pretty boring book, because that sort of thing tends to be pretty boring. So I had to work pretty hard to find things in it that were interesting to people. And one of the things was this. There was a whole boring section about how unless you do this thing, unless you include this little thing, dot this eye in this way, that there's a possibility that your will could be invalid, that your money wouldn't get left to the people that you intended to leave it to. So I had to take all that. And the bullet that I came up with was how to avoid accidentally disinheriting your heirs. In other words, how to avoid your children not getting the money that you think they're going to get, or whoever you want to leave your money to. Again, that's kind of unexpected. How to cure cancer with, I don't know how you say it here, like aluminum foil, with tin foil. How to cure cancer with a pen. You don't expect a cancer cure to be a pen. You don't expect it to be paper. You don't expect it to be different things. You always want to have that element of surprise, because that's what generates curiosity. Wow, how do I cure cancer with a pen? And then, of course, the way you cure cancer with a pen is by writing, you get negative emotions out. So a lot of people, when they journal, for instance, journaling, can be very healthy. Writing down your feelings, writing down your emotions, writing down what happened to you during the day. And when your emotions are healthy, you're less likely to get cancer. And if you're trying to heal yourself from cancer, that can be a big help for people, is that emotional healing. It can help their body's immune system get healthy and attack the cancer cells, and helps you get rid of the cancer. So what's unexpected about that is just that thing about the pen. So you just take that element and say, how did cure cancer with a pen? All of a sudden, it becomes interesting. All of a sudden, the person is intrigued and wants to get the report, wants to find out about that. Does that make sense? Can you see how you might do that, use the unexpected like that? The next is L, loads of details. A lot of times in bullets, the details about something are what make it interesting. You know, the detail of using a pen for a cure. It's not enough to say, how to cure cancer. You want to say, how to do it with a pen, how to do it in 15 minutes, how to, you know, the trick for when you fire people, you have to say these three words, right? You want to get numbers in there. You want to get specifics. These are the sort of things that make people feel that it's real, that your answer is real, that the information you have for them is real. And then limited is the other L. You don't want people to feel this is common knowledge. You know, you want to tell them, you know, the secret of what to never eat on an airplane, that the way to heal your arthritis that most doctors don't know, most patients don't know. This information is not available to anyone. It's only available here. It's limited in quantity. It's limited in time. What you need to know before you buy your next car. Like it's important, you have to do it now. You have to have that urgency in it. And by having limits, you start to create that urgency within that bullet. Extreme. So extreme in the sense that, you know, it's out on this end, it's out on that end. What never to eat on an airplane. What always to do, anytime you go on an airplane. You know, the best thing you can do if you have arthritis. The worst thing you can do if you have arthritis. If you have arthritis, this will make your pain twice as bad. Whoa, I want to know what that is. What's the worst thing I could do when I have arthritis? Maybe I'm doing it. Maybe I'm making my arthritis worse. I got to find out what this is. So you see how going to an extreme never, always, the one, it makes it so much more interesting. Tea is for testimonial or testimony. In other words, proof. People testifying that it's true. You know, millions, thousands of people are doing this. People are using it and you don't know it. This is a way to get discounts. Thousands of people use it. Why aren't you using it? This had a cure cancer with a pen. Harvard University study shows it really works. Well, now it's got more proof. Now it's got more credibility. Now I got to find out what it is. Gosh, Harvard University did a study and found out that this thing is true. So you want to build in a little bit of proof into your bullet if you can. And you can't, obviously, you can't always use all of these things in every bullet, in every headline, but just kind of going through it, thinking about, oh, how can I add limited? How can I make it a little more urgent? Ooh, how can I make a little more benefit? Maybe I didn't use that enough. How can I make it a little more limited? Like, ooh, it's a secret. Not many people know about it. And then the last one is to supersize it, to make it somehow bigger. And a lot of times I'll do this at the end of a bullet. Blah, blah, blah, how you pin, blah, blah, blah. So easy your grandma can do it. You know, bullet, you know, what never to eat on an airplane. Millions of people die every year from doing this. Something at the end also that gives it emotion. A lot of times it's good to say something, how to do something, how to get big discounts. How will your family feel when you take them to Disneyland and you only pay half price? You know, it's like, oh, now there's emotion in there. Now there's some feeling. Now you've reminded me that not only do I have to do it because it's a good idea, but I should do it, I should get this information because there's an emotional component to it. I won't feel good if I don't do it. I'll feel like not a good person. I'm not doing my best for my family. I'm not doing my best for myself. Or other people are doing it and I feel like I wanna keep up with other people. What do you mean other people are getting discounts? What do you mean other people, you know, get in free to places? You know, I wanna do that too. That's not fair they're doing that. So at the end of the bullet, sometimes you can just add that, think about how you can add that little bit of emotionalness. That one that I told you about the, what was it, lawyers had how to avoid accidentally disinheriting your heirs, right? So at the end of that, you might say, lawyers don't tell you about this because they make a lot of money by fixing this mistake after you've done it. And now it's like, oh, now I really gotta find this out because I don't want some lawyer. See how it adds that emotional twist to it. What I'd like to do is talk a little bit about, about headlines. So we talked a little bit about how we can take a bullet and make it into a headline, right? What never to eat on an airplane, bullet, what never to eat on an airplane, headline. So you say, what never to eat on an airplane, dear friend, I wanna tell you about this secret thing that you should never eat on an airplane. It could be very harmful to you. Airline people don't tell you about it. You can read it in the special report that comes with your newsletter on page 17. However, there's also many other important things that you need to know about in this wonderful newspaper, in this wonderful newsletter. I'll tell you all sorts of other things that could impact your health. And then you're off and running in terms of your copy, but you've led into it with a headline that's actually a bullet. Then you've broadened it out a little bit. So as I said though, sometimes headlines are what we call a hook to get you into something. Like my favorite one, my friend John Carleton, once had to do an ad for a golf product in the United States. I don't know about here, but in the United States we have a lot of people, they sell products to help you be a better golfer. And he was trying to figure out, oh, what should I write? How many times can I write? Hit the ball farther, make your putts more accurate. I've wrote about that so many times. What's new about this product? And he talked to the client, he talked to the client, and the client said, oh, there's really nothing new about this, it's the same old stuff. Just write me a great letter for it. John kept digging and digging, come on, there must be something, there must be something. How has this developed? What happened? How did this come about? Oh, same old stuff, found a better way to do this, blah, blah, blah. They said, well, wait, there is one thing. When I was developing this product, one of the ways, one of the things that went into it was I saw this golfer, right? And he only had one leg. And he was just on one leg, and he hit the ball, like so incredibly far. And the way he was standing, the way he was balancing on one leg, I realized that if I took that same balance, that same center of gravity, that same control, that there were certain things about that, that if I did it that way too, I could hit the ball even farther. So John realized that that was gold. He had been digging and he finally struck gold. And he came up with this classic ad, secrets of a one-legged golfer that enable you to drive farther, hit the putt more accurately, et cetera, et cetera, et cetera. So all of a sudden it became one of the most successful ads in that market, just by having found out that one thing and then incorporating it into the headline. So it became like one-legged golfer, I'm trying to make a hook, right? It's like a hook where you're fishing and the hook that you want the fish to bite into. I'm not a very good artist, but that's the idea. It's like you make this hook and it's like, one-legged golfer, help me golf better. Oh, I wanna bite on that. I wanna, and so you wanna find that, one of the ways to find headlines, not the only way. But one way is to look in your products for some kind of a hook. There was a newsletter that someone in America wrote and they had learned a lot of what they knew about how to think about investing. They learned it by being a poker player, by gambling. And that's one of the reasons we're going gambling tonight. But they had come from that background. So they learned about risk and they learned about how you bet, how you bet on cards, but also how do you bet on a stock? That's what stock picking is, is you're betting on a stock. So the promotions, the ads, the headlines, a lot of times would say, the poker playing secrets that enable you to be a better investor, or how one of the world's best poker players became one of the world's best investors. So you feel how interesting that is? Like, boy, I want to read that. It's like a one-legged golfer. It's like a poker player turns into a stock picker. You do that a little bit, Sonia, with your product, where you have these four people, that's unique. It's four people, they're working for you. This is a hook a little bit. Like you could have four people, four different parts of the world that are being covered. So sometimes you really have to dig for that. You really have to ask the editor, how did you start doing this? What's your background? What made you get into this? So you can find that one-legged golfer. You can find that little hook that, oh, I used to be a poker player. Oh, we didn't know this. We can use this in our advertising. So that's another thing that you can do. The third thing, kind of an umbrella. You can say, oh, this cures arthritis. This, we have this. We have this. We never need to have a plane. But sometimes it's what the newsletter is all about. Like one time I did a headline for a health newsletter. And the headline was, how to cure any disease by taking advantage of its hidden weakness. So the idea was that every, because when I read the stuff about alternative medicine, it seemed like they were always trying to find what is it about this disease that's like a little bit of a hidden weakness that we can use to cure the disease in a different way, not just with medicine, but with herbs or with doing certain things. Like for instance, cancer. Cancer loves sugar. Cancer has to eat. It has a very voracious appetite because it grows. It multiplies so much. So cancer's hidden weakness is that you cut off the sugar supply, it dies because it needs that sugar to grow. So some of the ways, the alternative ways of healing cancer are to figure out ways to starve it to death by cutting off its sugar supply because it needs so much sugar. So that's a great example of finding something interesting and intriguing to say about your product and using that as a headline rather than like we talked about the first one. This is using something very specific. One little thing. This is kind of an umbrella of this is the kind of things you're going to find, right? Kind of things that are going to transform you as an HR director, the kinds of things that are going to change your life as an investor. Then the other thing in a headline is empathy, right? And a lot of the ideas that we've been talking about when we talked about how to make headlines from understanding the product, a lot of the best headlines I thought that we came up with were things that showed that we had empathy and understanding for the prospect. We understand what it's like to be an administrator and never feel appreciated. We understand what it's like to be an investor and feel like you're not safe when you want to be safe. We understand that. So the headline shows an understanding of that. If you're tired of feeling unsafe all the time, here's a newsletter for you. To every secretary, to every administrative assistant that feels underappreciated, here's important news. It's like, oh, they understand me. This is something that understands who I am and understands me in a way other people don't. So now I'm sucked into the copy. And like we talked about yesterday, we just want to get you to read what's under the headline, not trying to sell you on anything in the headline. This is kind of related to empathy. It's true of everything we write. We want it to be about you, the prospect, not about me, not about my product. It's about you. It's about your struggles as an investor. It's about your feeling unappreciated as an administrative assistant. It's about your feeling frustrated as a purchasing agent. And you're feeling uncertainty. It's about you and your arthritis and how painful it is and how it hurts when you get up in the morning and how you can't sleep at night because you can't roll over because it hurts when you roll over. You can't open jars because it hurts when you open jars. It's about that. So a headline from that can be something like, never cringe again when you open a jar. Never have another sleepless night. It's about them. It's not about my wonderful product.
